FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study at baseline (9-10 years) and 1-year follow-up, N=9,066

FT_IC2.evidence: Social screen time (social media, texting, video chatting) and nonsocial screen time (YouTube, video games) measured separately as trichotomized weekday and weekend variables

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regressions: psychiatric symptoms predicting screen time (weekday nonsocial OR=1.22, 95% CI 1.22-1.23; weekend nonsocial OR=1.10, 95% CI 1.09-1.10; clinical rule-breaking predicting weekday nonsocial OR=1.65, 95% CI 1.63-1.67). Also bidirectional: screen time predicting future psychiatric symptoms

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple odds ratios with confidence intervals reported throughout: OR=1.22 (95% CI 1.22-1.23), OR=1.10 (95% CI 1.09-1.10), OR=1.65 (95% CI 1.63-1.67)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis: psychiatric symptoms at baseline predicting changes in screen time at 1-year follow-up, controlled for baseline screen use

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology, 2024), not a preprint or conference abstract

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological predictors (internalizing, externalizing, attention symptoms; ADHD symptoms; clinical levels of psychiatric problems). FT-EC7 only applies when predictors are EXCLUSIVELY demographic/contextual (SES, race, sex, age, etc.). This paper predicts screen time from psychiatric symptom measures, which are individual psychological characteristics, not demographics.
notes: Bidirectional associations observed; magnitudes comparable in both directions. Paper explicitly tests both psychiatric symptoms→screen time AND screen time→psychiatric symptoms pathways.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, approximately 12,000 participants aged 9-13 years at baseline, 2-year follow-up (n=455 at 2-year follow-up from initial n=707)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use types measured separately: TV watching (TVMs), Video (watching videos), Game (video games), Text (cell phone texting), SNS (social networking sites), VC (video chatting); also reading time. Platform-specific measurements extractable from analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects (LME) models and Mendelian randomization analyses reporting causal effects. Regression coefficients for screen use on cognitive/behavioral outcomes: TVM on picture vocabulary β=-2.39 (corrected p<0.02), TVM on withdrawn/depressed β=0.57 (corrected p<0.05), VC on behavioral problems β=-0.30 (corrected p<0.0006)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple effect coefficients with p-values reported: β=-2.39 (corrected p<0.02), β=0.57 (corrected p<0.05), β=-0.30 (corrected p<0.0006), and numerous Mendelian randomization coefficients in Table S2 with credible intervals

FT_IC5.evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis using ABCD data with causal inference methods (LME models, genetic instrumental variables, Mendelian randomization, two-sample MR analysis)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Advanced Science, 2024), not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports multiple screen use→outcome associations (screen time predicting cognitive and behavioral outcomes), and includes both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses. FT-EC7 would only apply if screen use were exclusively the dependent variable with only demographic predictors in all analyses. Instead, this paper primarily uses screen use/reading as exposures predicting brain development, cognition, and behavior outcomes.
notes: Causal inference emphasis; uses both mediation analysis (reading habits mediate screen use→brain volume) and displacement theory framework. MR analysis employed to investigate causal directionality.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, years 2-4 data, N=6,143 adolescents (mean age year-2=12 years), longitudinal follow-up design

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures video game addiction (VGAQ outcome) with reward processing (neural fMRI activation in caudate) as predictor. Video games are explicitly listed under NOT QUALIFYING exposures (E17: 'Console or PC video games' excluded). No smartphone or social media variable measured. Gaming addiction questionnaire is not a qualifying SM/phone exposure per criteria.

FT_IC3.evidence: Fails FT_IC2; exposure (reward processing neural activation) is neither smartphone nor social media

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2 (no smartphone/SM exposure); FT_EC7 inapplicable
notes: Paper focuses on video games (console/gaming device) measured via addiction questionnaire with neural reward processing as exposure. Does not meet smartphone/social media exposure requirements. Excluded at FT_IC2.
